title: Ensure there is only one active access key for any single IAM user

input_row.title: Ensure there is only one active access key for any single IAM user
input_row.description: Access keys are long-term credentials for an IAM user or the AWS account 'root' user. You can use access keys to sign programmatic requests to the AWS CLI or AWS API (directly or using the AWS SDK)
input_row.rationale: One of the best ways to protect your account is to not allow users to have multiple access keys.
input_row.audit: From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM dashboard at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. In the left navigation panel, choose Users. 3. Click on the IAM user name that you want to examine. 4. On the IAM user configuration page, select Security Credentials tab. 5. Under Access Keys section, in the Status column, check the current status for each access key associated with the IAM user. If the selected IAM user has more than one access key activated, then the user's access configuration does not adhere to security best practices, and the risk of accidental exposures increases. • Repeat steps 3-5 for each IAM user in your AWS account. From Command Line: 1. Run list-users command to list all IAM users within your account: aws iam list-users --query "Users[*].UserName" The command output should return an array that contains all your IAM user names. 2. Run list-access-keys command using the IAM user name list to return the current status of each access key associated with the selected IAM user: Internal Only - General aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <user-name> The command output should expose the metadata ("Username", "AccessKeyId", "Status", "CreateDate") for each access key on that user account. 3. Check the Status property value for each key returned to determine each key's current state. If the Status property value for more than one IAM access key is set to Active, the user access configuration does not adhere to this recommendation; refer to the remediation below. • Repeat steps 2 and 3 for each IAM user in your AWS account.
input_row.remediation: From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM dashboard at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. In the left navigation panel, choose Users. 3. Click on the IAM user name that you want to examine. 4. On the IAM user configuration page, select Security Credentials tab. 5. In Access Keys section, choose one access key that is less than 90 days old. This should be the only active key used by this IAM user to access AWS resources programmatically. Test your application(s) to make sure that the chosen access key is working. 6. In the same Access Keys section, identify your non-operational access keys (other than the chosen one) and deactivate it by clicking the Make Inactive link. 7. If you receive the Change Key Status confirmation box, click Deactivate to switch off the selected key. 8. Repeat steps 3-7 for each IAM user in your AWS account. From Command Line: 1. Using the IAM user and access key information provided in the Audit CLI, choose one access key that is less than 90 days old. This should be the only active key used by this IAM user to access AWS resources programmatically. Test your application(s) to make sure that the chosen access key is working. 2. Run the update-access-key command below using the IAM user name and the non-operational access key IDs to deactivate the unnecessary key(s). Refer to the Audit section to identify the unnecessary access key ID for the selected IAM user Note - the command does not return any output: Internal Only - General aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id <access-key-id> --status Inactive --user-name <user-name> 3. To confirm that the selected access key pair has been successfully deactivated run the list-access-keys audit command again for that IAM User: aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <user-name> • The command output should expose the metadata for each access key associated with the IAM user. If the non-operational key pair(s) Status is set to Inactive, the key has been successfully deactivated and the IAM user access configuration adheres now to this recommendation. 4. Repeat steps 1-3 for each IAM user in your AWS account.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can fully be verified using AWS APIs/boto3/AWS CLI commands. 
- It relates specifically to IAM users and access keys, which are standard AWS resources with available APIs.
- The audit field contains comprehensive CLI commands to check the configuration, and the control is entirely about IAM service properties.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_single_active_access_key_compliance_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use AWS CLI to list all IAM users:
   ```bash
   aws iam list-users --query "Users[*].UserName"
   ```
   This command returns an array of IAM usernames in the account.
   
2. Loop through each IAM user and retrieve their access keys using:
   ```bash
   aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <user-name>
   ```
   This checks the status of each access key associated with that user.

3. For each user, check the `Status` property of the returned access key information. The expected output should only have one key with `Status` set to `Active`. If more than one access key is found with `Active` status, it fails the compliance check.

4. Pass/Fail criteria:
   - If a user has more than one `Active` access key: **Fail**
   - If a user has one or zero `Active` access keys: **Pass**

5. Document results to indicate compliance for each IAM user concerning the requirement of having only one active access key. 

By following this structured approach, the compliance check can be conducted automatically on all IAM users in the AWS environment.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification as Automated is correct. This control can be effectively verified using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI, as it involves querying IAM user properties and access key statuses, which are accessible via AWS services.
- The AWS CLI commands provided are capable of retrieving the necessary information to check compliance with this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly accurate. The CLI commands used (`aws iam list-users` and `aws iam list-access-keys`) are valid and appropriate for retrieving IAM user information and access key statuses.
- The logic for checking the `Status` of access keys is correct, and the pass/fail criteria are clearly defined.
- The program name `aws_iam_user_single_active_access_key_compliance_check` is descriptive but could be more concise. It does follow the convention but could be improved for clarity.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Consider using boto3 for a more programmatically robust solution, which can handle exceptions and errors more gracefully than shell scripting with AWS CLI.
- The program name could be shortened to `aws_iam_single_active_access_key_check` for brevity while maintaining clarity.
- Ensure that the script or program handles pagination in case of a large number of IAM users or access keys.
- Consider logging or outputting detailed results for each user, including the number of active and inactive keys, for better audit traceability.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured, and the approach is sound. The use of AWS CLI commands is appropriate for this compliance check, and the logic is straightforward.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Consider implementing the check using boto3 for better error handling and flexibility. Update the program name for brevity and clarity as suggested.
- The assessment is technically sound, but these improvements can enhance robustness and readability.
